verdict_reasoning: BAX is a cytoplasmic/cytosolic protein in healthy cells. Its dominant localization is the cytosol as a soluble monomer; upon apoptotic stimuli it translocates to and inserts into the mitochondrial outer membrane (OMM) to induce MOMP. All membrane activity is restricted to intracellular membranes (OMM, and minor ER fractions). BAX has no plasma membrane targeting, no GPI anchor, no classical single-pass TM domain directing it to the cell surface, no CD designation, and no documented surface pool accessible from the extracellular face. The membrane it inserts into upon activation is the mitochondrial outer membrane — an intracellular, not plasma, membrane. No cell-surface-directed therapeutic antibody or CAR-T programs target BAX on the extracellular face. BH3-mimetics (venetoclax-class) are intracellular small molecules, not surface-directed agents. The 'wash test' fails: BAX has no mechanism to stably anchor to the outer leaflet of the plasma membrane. pMHC is the only potential surface story, which is excluded per scope. Therefore, BAX is classified as cytoplasmic/mitochondrial_internal with no extracellular surface accessibility.
